name: Suspension Reverse Fly
resistance_profile: Bodyweight horizontal abduction on suspension straps; load is greatest at the bottom stretch (arms forward, torso most horizontal) and eases as you open the arms — a lengthened-biased profile scaled by foot position.
cues: Body stays a rigid plank — don't let the hips sag.
Open the arms into a wide T, not up toward your face.
Lead with the pinkies and control the stretch on the way back.
execution_steps: Set suspension straps to mid-length and grasp a handle in each hand, palms facing each other.
Walk the feet forward and lean back with the arms extended straight in front, body straight from head to heels.
Brace the core and set a small, fixed bend in the elbows.
Pull your body up by opening the arms out to a wide 'T', leading with the pinkies until the hands are in line with the shoulders.
Lower slowly back to the extended, leaning-away stretch under full control.
good_when: You want a scalable, lengthened-biased bodyweight rear-delt option.
You train with a suspension trainer at home or outdoors.
You want to combine rear-delt work with whole-body core bracing.
bad_when: The strap anchor or handles are unstable and you cannot control the descent.
You need precise progressive loading — adjusting by foot angle is coarse.
common_errors: Bending the elbows so it becomes a row.
Letting the hips drop out of the plank.
Rushing the eccentric and losing the stretch.
progressions: Walk the feet further forward to make the body more horizontal and harder.
regressions: Walk the feet back to stand more upright.
Shorten the range and keep the torso higher.
